module: Module 2: In Search of a Universal Theory
content: So in the previous clip we talked about the ethics behind capitalism, the spirit of capitalism. Now we'll discuss according to Weber, where did the spirit of capitalism even come from? Now, the key thing is that this kind of spirit of capitalism, it's not enough if just one individual holds all these views, frugality, honesty, time is money, credit is money, all of that. It's not enough if just one person holds this. Capitalist system relies on economic transactions, so a group of people have to hold this view for it to originate. Now Weber, he looks at the concept of historical materialism. Now I'm going to ask you here to think about who talked about historical materialism. And let's hope you are able to connect the dots that Marx came up with the idea of historical materialism. So Weber now, because Marx wrote his stuff about 100 years ago. So Weber is actually kind of making fun of this idea and saying that this idea of historical materialism is naive. Because he says, the birthplace of capitalism where capitalism first flourished the New England colonies, Massachusetts, Pennsylvania, all those areas, this ideology existed before capitalism flourished, and these New England colonies were founded by preachers and graduates of seminaries. You know, really very deeply religious people and it was established for religious reasons. It was not established for commercial reasons. They came because they were fleeing persecution from the other established traditional religion of Catholicism in Europe. They were fleeing that, and so that's why they had come to these New England colonies in the United States. And it was these preachers, not tradespeople, not, you know, people with large capital already they, that is where capitalism was born, and this ideology had to fight against really strong hostile forces in the ancient and middle ages where this type of philosophy of moneymaking interest growth was considered the lowest form of greed. It was seen as an attitude lacking in all self-respect and so noble people. The courtiers, they, you know, they considered it very much beneath them to engage in any kind of commercial activity. It was very shameful for a noble family to go into some kind of commercial transaction. And this is not because the people in ancient and middle ages were, you know, very altruistic and they were not greedy. That is not at all true. The greed for gold existed since time immemorial. In fact, according to Weber greed, and unscrupulous in the pursuit of moneymaking is actually a specific characteristic of pre-capitalist society. The capitalist society has to fight against that for it to be get established. So the lack of conscience where the worker shirks their duties, where you have to have someone observing the worker all the time to make sure that they do their job. This attitude that I will do the minimum possible for me to survive. I'm not going to take initiative and do extra. I'm going to do the way things were done before. I'm not going to try all these new things. So according to Weber, those value systems, those ideologies are actually the biggest impediment to capitalism. So justice, capitalism cannot really use a worker who shirks their duties. Capitalism also cannot use a businessman who is unscrupulous in her dealings, and so capitalistic development did not come from greed and the making of money at any cost. In fact, this type of traditionalism is the fundamental enemy that capitalism has to overcome for it to be established. So what is this traditionalist view? Weber gives an example. So when, let's say it's a farming society, and when gathering a harvest, that's when you need the greatest number of workers and speed is very essential. Now, this speed can mean the difference between profit and heavy loss. And so workers are paid more per hour during these times with the idea that, okay, if I pay more per hour, then I'll attract more workers and also my existing workers will work longer. However, in traditional societies, one of the biggest problems is that increasing the hourly rate actually reduces the availability of labor. Because the workers think, okay, I need only this much to survive. Before you were paying me a per hour rate of 100 rupees, now you are paying me 120. So I need to work less hours because I need only this much to survive, and once I have this much, I can relax and enjoy life. And so this is the natural, traditional way of living to work enough to survive and live in a way that is comfortable. By nature according to Weber, humans do not wish to earn more and more and more money. Now, this attitude, according to Weber, is the primary roadblock faced by capitalism when it tries to increase the productivity of human labor by incentivizing it, it runs into this roadblock of this traditionalist attitude. So the opposite approach is to pay less so that the worker has to do work more and more to survive. So then you have sufficient labor. But Weber says this actually doesn't work because low wages usually result in low quality work. And you run into workers who have a traditionalist attitude where they are unwilling to learn new things, work with new me machinery, new technology. They wanna insist on doing things the way it was done, the traditional way. So you will run across craft type workers who will say, you know, five generations of my family have done it this way. Now you are introducing this new missionary I, you know, this goes against all tradition, all history, so I will not do it. So, according to Weber, all of these are big roadblocks, a traditionalist view held in precap society, which capitalism has to fight against to establish itself. So what capitalism really needs for it to be established is an attitude which says, what is the maximum I can do with the minimum of comfort? What it really needs are workers who work as if their work was a calling, an end in itself, diligent, requiring no manager, no oversight because they themselves are motivated to work to the best of their ability. And this idea where the worker believes that their work is their calling, it does not come naturally. It comes only from a long process of education and some kind of ideology. Of course, Weber recognizes that now because capitalism is spread everywhere, this attitude is everywhere, and so we take it for granted. But in the past, capitalists faced this issue of having to deal with this traditionalist mindset everywhere. Now, according to Weber, these Protestant workmen, now remember Protestantism was came up as a movement against Catholicism. So these Protestant workmen who had this idea that work is a calling from God. They were actually facing persecution everywhere because they, this was a new revolutionary movement, and so the traditionalist workers actually came and destroyed their tools and persecuted them, and that's why many of them fled from Europe to the United States. And it was not, according to Weber, the most opulent, the richest courts of Italy and France. That's not where the shift happened. It happened in these small, new townships, quite poor by comparison, in these New England colonies and in some other pockets in Europe. So this again, is to Weber it shows that it is not where capital was already there in the most opulent and richest courts, that's not where capitalism was formed. It was formed in these relatively poorer areas where they did not have this accumulation of capital already. So this philosophy of rationalism where there is this continuous focus on rigorously looking at how can I become more and more efficient? How can I produce more, how can I produce a better quality product and this continuous focus on improvement, looking at the data and then improving even further and investing towards accomplishing more and more efficiency and more and more scale. So this kind of philosophy of rationalism, it had to fight against all these other philosophies, this hand to mouth existence of the peasant do only with what is enough to survive. And the privileged traditionalism of the craftsmen where you create one artistic product, because that's what generations of artisans have done rather than create 100 manufactured ones using some machinery, or the feudal, the courtesans, the nobles, where they were exploiting political opportunities. Or just sheer speculation and adventurism where you go on these voyages and you kind of invade somebody and get their wealth. So these were all the ways of living in the ancient and middle ages and capitalism had to really go against all of these kind of ways of thinking and living. So according to Weber, the spirit of capitalism is best understood as part of the development of rationalism as a whole. However, he says that this development of rationalism cannot fully explain the spirit of capitalism because there is an irrational element in within the spirit of capitalism. This irrational element is this idea that once work is a calling that we exist to work which is irrational from a very narrow, self-interested point of view. So the spirit of capitalism needs this irrational element where not just the capitalist, but even the workers, they must perform their work as if it were an absolute end in itself, a calling. So where does this idea come from? And now this is where Weber tells us about the ideological basis for it. Where does this irrational idea come from? And we will look at that in the next subsection.
